revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:掛け流し]]
'''Kakenagashi''' (掛け流し) is a method of supplying and discharging water to the {{ill|Furo|en|Furo|qid=Q2637646|lt=Furo|1=Furo}} of a {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|qid=Q655311|lt=Onsen|1=Onsen}}. In this method, hot spring water—whether naturally emerging from the ground (natural emergence), self-emerging after drilling (drilled self-emergence), or mechanically pumped from a {{ill|hot spring source|ja|源泉|1=Spring Mouth|1=Spring Mouth|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hot spring source|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Mouth|qq=}} in the ground (drilled mechanical pumping)—is directly supplied to the bathtub, and the water that overflows is discharged without being reused. It is also known as '''gensen Flowing Bath'''.
[[File:Kaike onsen06s3648.jpg|thumb|260px|Example of Flowing Bath: hot spring continuously overflowing from the bathtub]]
== History of Kakenagashi == The original method of utilizing hot springs—natural emergence and drilled self-emergence—has long been in use and was essentially in a Flowing Bath state, so the term itself was unnecessary. Even after the advent of the {{ill|recirculating bath|ja|循環風呂|1=Recirculating Baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/recirculating bath|13=User:Immanuelle/Recirculating Baths|qq=}}, Flowing Bath did not immediately attract attention.
The first notable attention to Flowing Bath arose during the Legionella scare that occurred from {{ill|2000|en|2000|qid=Q1985|lt=2000|1=2000}} to {{ill|2002|en|2002|qid=Q1987|lt=2002|1=2002}}, when the proliferation of {{ill|Legionella|en|Legionella|qid=Q158013|lt=Legionella bacteria|1=Legionella}} in recirculating systems installed in facilities such as the {{ill|day-use bath facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/day-use bath facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} led to fatal accidents. This incident drew attention to the traditional Flowing Bath method, which does not employ an internal recirculation system that can serve as a breeding ground for bacteria.
Subsequently, among hot spring enthusiasts, whether a facility employs Flowing Bath became an element in enjoying a hot spring experience. Following the hot spring misrepresentation issue ({{ill|hot spring misrepresentation issue|ja|温泉偽装問題|1=Onsen Fraud Controversy|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hot spring misrepresentation issue|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=}}) in {{ill|2004|en|2004|qid=Q2014|lt=2004|1=2004}}, inns and bathing facilities began to advertise that they offered source Flowing Bath to cater to customers’ preference for authentic hot spring water. Nowadays, even some {{ill|Condominium|en|Condominium|qid=Q161851|lt=condominium|1=Condominium}}s advertise that every unit’s bathroom features Flowing Bath.
In {{ill|2023|en|2023|qid=Q49622|lt=2023|1=2023}}, an incident was reported at a Flowing Bath hot spring where Legionella bacteria were detected at levels up to approximately 3700 times the standard maximum. The cause was the failure to adhere to the regulation—mandating that the water be replaced once a week and that {{ill|Chlorine|en|Chlorine|qid=Q688|lt=chlorine|1=Chlorine}} be added to maintain proper concentration—set by local authorities. The water flow in this Flowing Bath system was 70 liters per minute, demonstrating that even with a considerable water volume, conditions may still favor Legionella proliferation.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/20230228-OYT1T50173/ |title= 大浴場の湯入れ替え「盆と正月のみでいい」|publisher=読売新聞 |date=2023-02-28 |accessdate=2023-02-28}} :{{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230228061458/https://www.yomiuri.co.jp/national/20230228-OYT1T50173/|date=2023-02-28|title=大浴場の湯入れ替え「盆と正月のみでいい」}} </ref>
It is claimed by {{ill|Tadatoku Matsuda|ja|松田忠徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tadatoku Matsuda|1=Tadanori Matsuda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11532059}} in his book that he was the first to use the term "Flowing Bath." <ref>{{ill|2025|en|2025|qid=Q49616|lt=2025|1=2025}} February 11 broadcast on {{ill|TV Asahi|en|TV Asahi|qid=Q908436|lt=TV Asahi|1=TV Asahi}} and {{ill|Wide! Scramble|ja|ワイド!スクランブル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wide! Scramble|1=WIDE!SCRAMBLE|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11351181}} in the afternoon, where he appeared alongside {{ill|Shuzo Matsuoka|en|Shuzo Matsuoka|qid=Q978594|lt=Shuzo Matsuoka|1=Shuzo Matsuoka}} and was introduced as having used the term for the first time.</ref> There is also an opinion that the term "gensen Flowing Bath" was coined by {{ill|Etsuo Noguchi|ja|野口悦男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Etsuo Noguchi|1=Etsuo Noguchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11645168}}.<ref>NIKKEI NET「[http://waga.nikkei.co.jp/travel/travel.aspx?i=MMWAc1000026032008 正しい温泉は『源泉かけ流し』（野口悦男：第1回）]」 {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080627150839/http://waga.nikkei.co.jp/travel/travel.aspx?i=MMWAc1000026032008 |date=2008th year 6月27日 }}</ref><ref>[http://www.zakzak.co.jp/gei/200811/g2008112617_all.html 「源泉かけ流し」野口悦男さん急逝] ZAKZAK 2008th year 11月26日</ref>
== Characteristics and Efficacy == The hot spring water supplied to a Flowing Bath bathtub is often in a fresh state before deterioration or alteration begins upon exposure to air. This allows one to experience the original {{ill|mineral composition|ja|泉質|1=Hot Spring Types|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mineral composition|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|qq=}} (color, aroma, texture, etc.) of the source, and it often contains floating {{ill|hot spring deposits|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|1=Sinter|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hot spring deposits|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|qq=}}. Moreover, because disinfectant chemicals are generally not added, differences in texture and especially aroma become more apparent. These characteristics can serve as important factors in choosing a bathing facility, whether it be a {{ill|Hotel|en|Hotel|qid=Q27686|lt=hotel|1=Hotel}} or an {{ill|Ryokan|en|Ryokan|qid=Q562930|lt=inn|1=Ryokan}}.
To date, there is no conclusive evidence that bathing in a Flowing Bath-style system is more effective against diseases than using recirculating systems (in any case, widely circulated claims—such as those that certain water qualities are effective against {{ill|Rheumatism|en|Rheumatism|qid=Q684924|lt=rheumatism|1=Rheumatism}}—remain medically debated).
== Identification Methods == Basically, if water overflows from the bathtub, it indicates that an equal amount of water is being supplied from the spout, and if that water is hot spring water from the source, the system can be regarded as "(source) Flowing Bath."
While factors such as the intensity of color or aroma and the presence of floating hot spring deposits can offer clues, some hot springs exhibit qualities that are clear, colorless, tasteless, and odorless with no deposits. Thus, the determination is usually made by comparing the volume of water supplied from the spout to the volume overflowing from the bathtub’s edge. In particular, if a large volume of water is supplied yet little overflows, it is often due to the use of a recirculation system (in some cases, bathtubs are designed with built-in discharge pipes that allow water to be expelled without causing an overflow).
In {{ill|2005|en|2005|qid=Q2019|lt=2005|1=2005}}, the enforcement regulations of the {{ill|Hot Spring Law|ja|温泉法|1=Onsen Act|1=Onsen Act|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Law|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Act|qq=}} were revised, and bathing facilities using hot springs were mandated to display a {{ill|Hot Spring Analysis Report|ja|温泉分析書|1=Hot spring analysis report|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Analysis Report|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot spring analysis report|qq=}} that clearly states whether the following are used: * water addition, * heating, * circulation/filtration equipment, and * bath additives or disinfectants. These details, along with the condition of the bathtub, allow one to determine whether Flowing Bath is employed.
Furthermore, the {{ill|Japan Hot Spring Association|ja|日本温泉協会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Hot Spring Association|1=Japan Spa Association|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q60987269}} also provides, on a voluntary basis, a '''Natural Hot Spring Display Board''' at each facility that lists whether circulation, filtration, or water addition is used. These measures were introduced in the early 2000s in response to social issues such as the use of bath additives in hot spring facilities and the proliferation of Legionella bacteria in facilities employing recirculation systems, as well as misleading practices like: * referring to a mere tap water bath as a "hot spring," and * advertising "100% source" despite the addition of water.
== Footnotes ==
<references />
== Related Topics == {{Wiki Portal Link|hot spring}} * {{ill|tarenagashi|ja|垂れ流し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tarenagashi|1=Q11425793|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11425793}} * {{ill|Mineral water|en|Mineral water|qid=Q178921|lt=mineral water|1=Mineral water}} * {{ill|hot spring deposits|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|1=Sinter|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hot spring deposits|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|qq=}}
== External Links == * [https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/ Protection and Use of Hot Springs (Environmental Agency, Nature Environment Bureau)] * [http://www.spa.or.jp/ Japan Hot Spring Association] * [http://www.gensen-Flowing Bath.jp/ Japan Gensen Kakenagashi Hot Spring Association]
{{Onsen}}
